cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the answer here. So, the question is about Mr. Williams being stopped by a policeman. Let me read through the context again to make sure I get all the details. So, the story says that Mr. Williams was driving home and came to a crossroad with a "SLOW" sign. He slowed down, looked both ways, and then drove across without stopping completely. Then he heard a police whistle, pulled over, and the policeman said he didn't stop at the crossing. Mr. Williams argues that the sign only said "SLOW" and he did go slow. The policeman then realizes he's in the wrong street, implying maybe he was supposed to stop. The question is: When he was stopped by a policeman, he ___. The choices are: A) was driving at a high speed B) was driving onto the main road C) was going to stop his car D) was driving slowly Let me think. The key part is when Mr. Williams approached the crossroad. The sign said "SLOW" which means drivers should slow down but don't need to stop unless there's traffic on the main road. He did slow down and looked both ways, then drove across without stopping. The policeman accused him of not stopping, but Mr. Williams said he didn't need to stop because of the sign. However, the policeman then says he's in the wrong street. Wait, maybe the sign was actually supposed to be a "STOP" sign, but the policeman got it wrong? Or perhaps the crossroad where Mr. Williams was actually required to stop had a "SLOW" sign? Maybe there was a mistake in the sign or the policeman's knowledge. But the question is about what Mr. Williams was doing when he was stopped. The story says he slowed down, looked both ways, and then drove across without stopping completely. So at the moment he was stopped by the policeman, he was already on the main road? Or was he in the process of driving onto the main road? Let me check the exact wording: "He slowed down when he came to the main road, looked both ways to see that nothing was coming, and then drove across without stopping completely." So he came to the main road (the crossroad), slowed down, looked both ways, and then drove across. So "drove across" would mean he entered the main road. So when the policeman stopped him, he was driving onto the main road. So that would be choice B. But let me check the other options. Choice D says he was driving slowly. Well, he did slow down, but the issue is whether he stopped. The sign said "SLOW", so he was supposed to slow, not necessarily stop. But the policeman thought he didn't stop. But the question is about what he was doing when he was stopped. At the moment he was stopped, he had already passed the crossroad, but he was driving onto the main road. So the answer is B. Alternatively, maybe when the policeman blew the whistle, Mr. Williams was still in the process of driving onto the main road. The story says "At once he heard a police whistle, so he pulled into the side of the road and stopped." So maybe he was already on the main road when he was stopped. So driving onto the main road would be B. Wait, but "drove across without stopping completely" – so he entered the main road and was driving across it, so he was on the main road. The policeman then pulled him over. So when he was stopped, he was driving onto the main road (B) or was driving slowly (D)? The question is about his action when he was stopped. The story says he was driving slowly when he approached the crossroad. Then he drove across (so he was on the main road), then he heard the whistle. So when he was stopped by the policeman, he was driving onto the main road. So B is correct. D
